text: So this is a video just specifically of setting, how I set up my isoparastheltic anastomosis. A lot of people use V-Lock, I like to use Vicryl, and I'll show you why in a second. So basically what I do is I set up these Vicryl stay sutures on either side of my future enterotomy. I make a square knot, and I convert it to a slip, and then convert it back to square. And then I just make sure that I get that right tension on each stitch. And then I leave that tail, because I'm going to use that in just a bit. So then I estimate about twelve millimeters, the size of the stapler, away from the last stitch. That's gonna give me two nice stay sutures to control the eventual enterotomies and to help me put the stapler through.

The surgeon creates enterotomies while controlling both edges with previously placed Vicryl stay sutures. The stapler is inserted, and the surgeon dynamically aligns each corner onto the stapler, ensuring control over the staple anastomosis. Both ends of the staple line are visible, and the surgeon hooks and dunks the staple line under to secure the corner.

And so then I'm going to talk about a couple of the techniques I use. So I lift up and I push down right in front of my suture. And then I'm able to set the suture with that needle tip. And then I just keep going. Full thickness bites. And I just speed it up here just to get it through here. And then I do the same for this last little bit. So push that staple line in and dump it down. Then I come beyond this last stitch. I come beyond. the suture, and then I tie it to that previous suture's tie.

The surgeon cuts off both ends of the previous sutures and performs a Lembert stitch, placing one stitch behind and then running a Lembert suture below. The needle is used to set the suture with proper tension, pulling the stay suture up and securing it with stitches in front and behind.
